records.ety: [OE. acord, accord, OF. acort, acorde, F. accord, fr. OF. acorder, F. accorder. See Accord, v. t.]

defs: Agreement or concurrence of opinion, will, or action; harmony of mind; consent; assent.
quotes: A mediator of an accord and peace between them.
These all continued with one accord in prayer.

defs: Harmony of sounds; agreement in pitch and tone; concord; as, the accord of tones.
quotes: Those sweet accords are even the angels' lays.

defs: Agreement, harmony, or just correspondence of things; as, the accord of light and shade in painting.
Voluntary or spontaneous motion or impulse to act; -- preceded by own; as, of one's own accord.
quotes: That which groweth of its own accord of thy harvest thou shalt not reap.
Of his own accord he went unto you.

defs: An agreement between parties in controversy, by which satisfaction for an injury is stipulated, and which, when executed, bars a suit.
quotes: They rushed with one accord into the theater.

records.ety: [OE. acorden, accorden, OF. acorder, F. accorder, fr. LL. accordare; L. ad + cor, cordis, heart. Cf. Concord, Discord, and see Heart.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Accorded; p. pr. & vb. n. According.]

defs: To make to agree or correspond; to suit one thing to another; to adjust; -- followed by to.
quotes: Her hands accorded the lute's music to the voice.

defs: To bring to an agreement, as persons; to reconcile; to settle, adjust, harmonize, or compose, as things; as, to accord suits or controversies.
quotes: When they were accorded from the fray.
All which particulars, being confessedly knotty and difficult can never be accorded but by a competent stock of critical learning.

defs: To grant as suitable or proper; to concede; to award; as, to accord to one due praise.
To agree; to correspond; to be in harmony; -- followed by with, formerly also by to; as, his disposition accords with his looks.
quotes: My heart accordeth with my tongue.
Thy actions to thy words accord.

defs: To agree in pitch and tone.
